title: Foundation Habitat
body: The first permanent crew quarters of a lunar outpost: a rigid pressurized module housing four, with integral 5 g/cm² shielding mass and the base's core life-support volume.
realWorld: NASA's Foundation Surface Habitat concept and Italy's ASI Multi-Purpose Habitat, slated for the Moon Base Phase 3 architecture announced March 2026.

title: Deployed Solar Array
body: Roll-out photovoltaic wings sized at ~100 W/kg. Near the south-pole ridge sites, ~90% illumination makes solar the workhorse — until the long eclipse windows and the lunar night demand storage or nuclear backup. Dust accumulation degrades output over time.
realWorld: ISS Roll-Out Solar Array (ROSA) heritage, the deployable class baselined for Artemis surface power.

title: Battery Bank
body: Lithium-ion storage at ~200 Wh/kg with ~90% round-trip efficiency. Bridges short eclipses and day-side load spikes; hopeless for the 354-hour lunar night, where the required mass becomes prohibitive.
realWorld: Li-ion space-rated battery class, as flown on ISS power channel upgrades.

title: Regenerative Fuel Cell
body: Electrolyzes water with surplus day power, then runs the H2/O2 back through a fuel cell at night. Only ~55% efficient round trip, but its ~600 Wh/kg system energy density makes it the pre-fission answer to the lunar night.
realWorld: NASA regenerative fuel cell development for lunar night survival.

title: Fission Surface Power
body: A 40 kWe fission reactor rated for ten years of continuous output, indifferent to the day/night cycle. The keystone of any permanent outpost: it turns the lunar night from an existential threat into a power-budget line item. Requires a keep-out zone for radiation.
realWorld: NASA's Fission Surface Power RFP: 40 kWe for >= 10 years, <= 6,000 kg, within a 4 m x 6 m stowed envelope; Kilopower/KRUSTY (2018) demonstrated the technology at ~1 kWe.

title: RTG Keep-Alive Unit
body: A radioisotope generator producing a few hundred watts regardless of sunlight. Too small to run a base, but enough to keep critical electronics and heaters alive through the night — the difference between a dormant outpost and a dead one.
realWorld: Radioisotope power systems heritage from Apollo ALSEP through Mars rovers; NASA Moon Base Phase 1 lists RTG/RHU night-survival units.

title: Radiator Wing
body: Deployable panels that reject the base's waste heat to space. With no atmosphere, radiation is the only way to dump heat — and against the 400 K daytime surface it is hard work; the same panels reject ~1.6x more against the 100 K night sky.
realWorld: ISS Heat Rejection System deployable radiator wings.

title: Machine Components
body: Precision parts, electronics, fittings, and mechanisms — everything a lunar base cannot yet make for itself. Every kilogram rides a rocket from Earth, which is why import mass is the currency of early colonization.
realWorld: Represents the imported dry mass of all early-phase hardware; cost dominated by $/kg-to-surface launch tiers.

title: Water
body: Drinking, hygiene, food prep — and via electrolysis, breathable oxygen and rocket propellant. Each crew member needs 3.54 kg of potable water a day, roughly doubled by hygiene. The ISS recycles over 90% of it; so does your ECLSS core.
realWorld: NASA ALS: 3.54 kg/person-day potable. ISS ECLSS water recovery reached ~98% with the Brine Processor Assembly.

title: Wastewater
body: Urine, cabin humidity condensate, and hygiene gray water. Not waste at all — it is tomorrow's drinking water, minus the brine the recycler cannot yet reclaim.
realWorld: ISS processes all three streams; brine processing was the last few percent of closure.

title: Oxygen
body: 0.84 kg per crew member per day, every day, no exceptions. Early bases electrolyze it from water; Phase 3 pulls it straight out of regolith, which is 45% oxygen by mass.
realWorld: NASA ALS standard crew O2 consumption; ISS OGA generates 2.3-9 kg/day.

title: Carbon Dioxide
body: Each crew member exhales a kilogram a day. Unscrubbed, it accumulates in the cabin until headaches become unconsciousness. Scrubbed and stored, it is Sabatier feedstock that closes the oxygen loop.
realWorld: ISS CDRA scrubs CO2; Apollo 13's square-peg scrubber crisis is the cautionary tale.

title: Hydrogen
body: The light end of water. Electrolysis yields 0.11 kg of hydrogen per kilogram of water split — feed for the Sabatier reactor, ilmenite reduction, and eventually LH2 propellant.
realWorld: Stoichiometric 8:1 oxygen:hydrogen split by mass.

title: Methane
body: The Sabatier reaction's carbon sink. Early bases store or vent it; later ones liquefy it as engine propellant for methalox landers.
realWorld: ISS Sabatier vented its methane; Starship-class vehicles burn methalox.

title: Food
body: 0.62 kg of dry mass per person per day, all imported until the greenhouses arrive in Phase 3. The heaviest recurring line item on the manifest, and the first thing crews notice when it runs out.
realWorld: NASA ALS dry food mass for ~2,500-2,700 kcal/day.

title: Medical Kits
body: Pharmaceuticals, diagnostics, trauma supplies. A medical emergency 384,000 km from the nearest hospital is survivable exactly as often as the kit and the clinic make it.
realWorld: ISS carries extensive medical kits; lunar missions add surgical-lite capability.

title: Storm Shelter
body: A berm-covered refuge with at least 10 g/cm2 of regolith overhead. When the solar particle event warning sounds, everyone gets inside before the protons arrive — it turns a 500 mSv emergency into a 10 mSv inconvenience.
realWorld: 4 g/cm2 of regolith drops a major SPE below NASA's 30-day limit; 10 g/cm2 gives a factor-2 margin.

title: ECLSS Core
body: The machine the crew lives inside: CO2 scrubbing (8 kg/day), oxygen generation by electrolysis (9 kg/day), and a water processor that recovers 93% of every gray liter. When the power fails, this rack is why the clock starts ticking.
realWorld: ISS ECLSS rack set: CDRA scrubber, Oxygen Generation Assembly, Water Processor Assembly.

title: Water/Gas Storage
body: Tank farm unit holding five tonnes of water or one tonne of pressurized gas. Buffer capacity is survival margin: the difference between a late resupply being an annoyance or an obituary.
realWorld: Standard tank-farm consumable buffering.

title: Comms Tower
body: Direct-to-Earth link (1.28 light-seconds each way) plus a local LTE mast for rovers and automation. Mission control is never more than 2.6 seconds of round trip away — close enough for advice, too far for rescue.
realWorld: Nokia's lunar 4G/LTE system flew on Intuitive Machines IM-2 (2025).

title: Exercise Module
body: Resistive and aerobic machines that substitute for five-sixths of a person's missing weight. Two hours a day per crew member fully offsets bone and muscle loss — skip it and the Moon quietly dismantles your skeleton.
realWorld: ISS ARED (Advanced Resistive Exercise Device); 2 h/day is the ISS countermeasure standard.

title: Clinic
body: Exam bay, telemedicine link, and just enough surgical capability to matter. Heals the slow damage and blunts the sharp emergencies; serious cases still mean an evac burn home.
realWorld: Lunar-surface medical concepts extend ISS telemedicine with trauma capability.

title: Sabatier Unit
body: CO2 + 4H2 → CH4 + 2H2O, with heat to spare. Feeds scrubbed cabin CO2 and electrolysis hydrogen back into the water loop, recovering oxygen that would otherwise be lost — the reaction that closes the air loop.
realWorld: Flown on ISS from 2010; the methane was vented there, stored here.

title: Regolith
body: The Moon's crushed-glass topsoil: 45% oxygen by mass, locked in oxides. Free for the scooping, brutal on seals and lungs, and the feedstock for oxygen, metals, and printed structures alike.
realWorld: Loose bulk density ~1,500 kg/m³; sharp and electrostatically clingy because nothing has ever weathered it.

title: Water Ice
body: Billions of tonnes of comet-delivered water, cold-trapped in craters that haven't seen the Sun in two billion years. The reason every agency on Earth points at the lunar south pole.
realWorld: LCROSS measured 5.6 ± 2.9 wt% ice in Cabeus crater regolith (Colaprete et al. 2010).

title: Liquid Oxygen
body: Cryogenic oxidizer — roughly 80% of a rocket's propellant mass. Made locally from ice or regolith, it turns the colony from a cost center into a gas station at the top of Earth's gravity well.
realWorld: All-in production chain ~11.3 kWh/kg; 0.1%/day boiloff in surface tanks.

title: Metal Slag
body: The molten cathode product of regolith electrolysis: iron, silicon, aluminium, and titanium waiting for a refinery to tell them apart.
realWorld: MRE ferrosilicon route rated most mass-efficient in NASA/MIT trade studies.

title: Spent Regolith
body: What's left of the melt after the oxygen and metals are gone. Pile it on the berms.
realWorld: Process residue accounting for the mass balance of molten regolith electrolysis.

title: Printed Structure
body: Sintered regolith elements straight out of the printer: habitat shells, pad tiles, berm blocks. Every kilogram printed is a kilogram that didn't ride a rocket — the 60-80% build-cost discount that makes Phase 3 economics work.
realWorld: ESA/Foster+Partners D-Shape printed a 1.5 t block from simulant; ICON's Olympus melts regolith with lasers; Chang'e-8 will test it on the Moon.

title: Spare Parts
body: Bearings, seals, filters, boards. Machines wear out fast in vacuum and dust, and until the workshop comes online every replacement part is imported mass.
realWorld: Maintenance demand is the quiet killer of long-duration outposts — ECLSS spares dominated ISS logistics planning.

title: Field Science Lab
body: Instrument suite for geology, seismology, and dust studies. Science points fund the research tree — and the research tree is how you stop importing everything.
realWorld: Apollo ALSEP packages ran for years after the crews left.

title: Ice Harvester
body: A cold-rated excavator that works in permanent shadow at 40 K, eating icy regolith by the tonne. The first machine in the chain that ends with rocket propellant.
realWorld: Interlune's full-scale excavator prototype (2025) ingests 100 t of regolith per hour — this is its distant, polar-hardened cousin.

title: Volatile Oven
body: Bakes icy regolith until the water lets go, at 1.5 kWh per recovered kilogram. The dry tailings go back out the hopper.
realWorld: Thermal volatile extraction per NASA ISRU pilot-plant concepts.

title: Electrolyzer (industrial)
body: Splits water into 89% oxygen and 11% hydrogen by mass at ~5 kWh/kg. Upstream of everything: breathable air, fuel cells, Sabatier feedstock, and rocket propellant.
realWorld: Scaled PEM stack; the same chemistry as the ISS Oxygen Generation Assembly.

title: Cryo Plant
body: Compresses and chills oxygen gas into liquid at 90 K. The 11.3 kWh/kg all-in chain cost is why propellant export demands megawatt-class power — the sim will teach you this before the spreadsheet does.
realWorld: LOX liquefaction chain modeling per NASA propellant-production studies (SDD §11 validation target).

title: MRE Plant
body: Molten Regolith Electrolysis: melt the ground at 1,600 °C and run current through it — oxygen bubbles off the anode, metal pools at the cathode. No ice required; the highlands themselves are the ore.
realWorld: MIT/Schreiner sizing: a 400 kg, 14 kW plant makes ~1 t O₂/yr; a 1.6 t plant makes ~10 t/yr. About 280 kg of O₂ per 1,000 kg of regolith processed.

title: Regolith Printer
body: Additive construction at 2 kWh/kg: regolith in, structural elements out. Berms, pads, and eventually habitat shells — built from the Moon, for the Moon.
realWorld: ICON Olympus (NASA award) and ESA's D-Shape demos; China tests construction printing on Chang'e-8.

title: Regolith Berm
body: Twenty tonnes of bulldozed Moon piled against a wall. Adds ~6 g/cm² of areal density to adjacent structures — cheap, dumb, and the best radiation deal on the surface.
realWorld: Regolith shielding: >4 g/cm² tames a major SPE; berms and burial are the standard concepts.

title: Landing Pad
body: Sintered tiles that keep descent plumes from sandblasting the base. Cuts landing dust by 90% — your solar arrays will notice.
realWorld: Unmitigated plumes eject regolith at km/s; Apollo 12 sandblasted Surveyor 3 from 160 m away.

title: Propellant Depot Pad
body: A refueling apron where visiting landers buy locally made LOX. The first export revenue the colony ever earns — and the seed of the cislunar economy thesis.
realWorld: Lunar propellant depots are the recurring business case for ice mining: fuel sold at the top of the gravity well.

title: Greenhouse Module
body: Fifty square meters of LED-lit hydroponics — about one person's full diet. The lights, not the plants, are the power bill.
realWorld: NASA Veggie heritage scaled up; 40-50 m²/person for full caloric diets.

title: Agri-Dome
body: Five hundred square meters under one pressure shell: eleven diets, a CO₂ sink, and an O₂ spring. Closed-loop ecology at the scale where it starts getting Biosphere-2 temperamental.
realWorld: MELiSSA's compartmentalized loop needed 453 m² for six crew menus.

title: Medical Center
body: Surgery, imaging, intensive care. The building that removes 'evacuate to Earth' from the emergency flowchart.
realWorld: Settlement-scale extension of ISS telemedicine practice.

title: Refinery
body: Separates MRE slag into usable metal at 8 kWh/kg. Iron first; the rest of the melt waits for better chemistry.
realWorld: Slag-to-ingot processing per NASA ISRU metallurgy studies.

title: Workshop
body: Mills, lathes, and printers turning lunar iron into the spare parts that keep everything else alive. Every part made here is import mass deleted.
realWorld: The mass-closure keystone: maintenance localization.

title: Fabrication Plant
body: Precision manufacturing of machine components from local metal — only the electronics fraction still rides a rocket.
realWorld: Advanced manufacturing tier; electronics independence is a Phase-6 problem.

title: Water Reclamation Plant
body: Brine-processing at settlement scale pushes water recovery to 98% — the last few percent that ISS chased for two decades.
realWorld: ISS Brine Processor Assembly raised recovery from 93-94% to ~98%.

title: Printed Habitat Block
body: A pressurized apartment block under twenty g/cm² of printed regolith: twelve residents, storm-proof by construction, and mostly made of Moon.
realWorld: Regolith-shell habitat concepts from ESA/ICON printing programs.

title: Mass Driver
body: An electromagnetic rail that throws cargo into space without burning a gram of propellant. Megawatt-hungry, megaproject-priced — and it makes exports nearly free at the margin.
realWorld: O'Neill-era concept; 10 MW class when segments complete. Speculative engineering, sound physics.

title: Volatile Combine
body: Eats a hundred tonnes of regolith and exhales hydrogen, water, carbon — and about a gram and a half of helium-3. The most speculative machine on the Moon.
realWorld: Interlune/Vermeer demonstrated a full-scale 100 t/hr excavator in 2025; the economics remain unproven.

title: Solar Farm Field
body: Photovoltaics made from MRE silicon — power infrastructure that never rode a rocket. The endgame of in-situ manufacturing.
realWorld: In-situ PV fabrication from regolith silicon, a long-standing late-tech concept.

title: Beamed Power Pilot
body: Microwave power beamed to receivers in permanent shadow — mining the dark without dragging cables across the rim.
realWorld: Power-beaming demos for PSR operations.

title: Crater Dome Segment
body: A section of pressurized roof over an entire crater. Paraterraforming: the city stops living in buildings and starts living in geography. Firmly speculative.
realWorld: Paraterraforming literature (Richard Taylor et al.); no engineering program exists.

title: Civic Center
body: Council chamber, school, archive, plaza. When the referendum on autonomy finally comes, it happens here. Speculative era content.
realWorld: The social infrastructure of a settlement that has become a polity.

title: Iron
body: The Moon's first structural metal, refined from MRE slag. Beams, parts, machines — civilization's skeleton, locally sourced.
realWorld: Iron dominates the recoverable metal fraction of processed regolith.

title: Helium-3
body: A solar-wind isotope worth ~$20M/kg to Earth's dilution refrigerators and neutron detectors. The fusion story is hype for now — the cryogenics market is real but tiny.
realWorld: Interlune holds ~$500M in binding purchase orders; near-term demand is quantum-computing cryogenics, not reactors.
